subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus square of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> ) graph for a simple pendulum executing simple harmonic motion.
question: Choose the correct length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus square of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> ) graph for a simple pendulum executing simple harmonic motion.
solution:
A simple pendulum consists of a point mass attached to an inextensible string and suspended from a support.

The formula to calculate the time period T of a simple pendulum is given by

T=2πlg.......................(1)

where, l is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the relation between the time period and the length.

T2=4π2g×𝓁T2∝l............................(2)

Equation (2) suggests that the graph between the square of the time period versus the length of the pendulum is a straight line passing through the origin.

subject: Physics
q: The maximum potential energy of a block executing simple harmonic motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. A is amplitude of oscillation. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, the kinetic energy of the block is
question: The maximum potential energy of a block executing simple harmonic motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. A is amplitude of oscillation. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, the kinetic energy of the block is
solution:
The maximum potential energy of a block executing simple harmonic motion is given by Umax=12mω2A2=25 J

As, kinetic energy of particle executing SHM is KE=12mω2A2-x2.

So, kinetic energy of block KE at A2=12mv12=12mω2A2-A24

⇒KE=12mω23A24=3412mω2A2

⇒KE=34×25=18.75 J

\mathrm{l}_{\mathrm{eff}}$ decreases when the child stands up.

question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion represented by displacement function as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. If the position and velocity of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively, then its amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
x(t)=Asin(ωt+ϕ)

v(t)=Aωcos(ωt+ϕ)

2=Asinϕ ...1

2ω=Aωcosϕ  ...2

From (1) and (2)

tanϕ=1

ϕ=45°

Putting the value of ϕ in equation (1)

2=A12

A=22

x=2

q: A rod of mass <math> <mi>M</mi> </math> and length <math> <mrow> <mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi></mrow> </math> is suspended at its middle by a wire. It exhibits torsional oscillations. If two masses, each of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math>, are attached at a distance <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow> </math> from its centre on both sides, it reduces the oscillation frequency by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of ratio <math><mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>M</mi></mrow></mrow></math> is close to
question: A rod of mass <math> <mi>M</mi> </math> and length <math> <mrow> <mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi></mrow> </math> is suspended at its middle by a wire. It exhibits torsional oscillations. If two masses, each of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math>, are attached at a distance <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow> </math> from its centre on both sides, it reduces the oscillation frequency by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of ratio <math><mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>M</mi></mrow></mrow></math> is close to
solution:
Let C be the torsional constant of the wire.

f=12πCM2L212=12π3CM. L2

After masses are attached,

f′=12π CM.2L212+mL24×2

⇒0.8f=12π CM3+m2L2

⇒0.64×3CM=CM3+m2

0.64M+0.64×32m=M

⇒mM=0.37

q: Two massless springs with spring constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></math>, carry <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> masses at their free ends. These two masses oscillate vertically such that their maximum velocities are equal. Then, the ratio of their respective amplitudes will be :
question: Two massless springs with spring constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></math>, carry <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> masses at their free ends. These two masses oscillate vertically such that their maximum velocities are equal. Then, the ratio of their respective amplitudes will be :
solution:
Maximum velocity is given by vmax=ωA =kmA

Here, k1m1A1=k2m2A2

  ⇒2k50×10-3A1=9k100×10-3A2

  ⇒2A1=3A2

Thus, ratio of amplitude is A1A2=32=3:2.

question: In a simple harmonic oscillation, what fraction of total mechanical energy is in the form of kinetic energy, when the particle is midway between mean and extreme position.

=12 mω2 A2-A24

=12 mω23 A24

At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the speed of particle will be (Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>)
question: The equation of a particle executing simple harmonic motion is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>π</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the speed of particle will be (Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>)
solution:
Given, x=sinπt+π3

Velocity  v=dxdt=πcosπt+π3

Now, at t=1 s, v=πcosπ+π3

 v=-π2=-3.142=-1.57 m s-1⇒v=157 cm s-1

subject: Physics
q: Bob of a simple pendulum of length $l$ is made of iron. The pendulum is oscillating over a horizontal coil carrying direct current. If the time period of the pendulum is $\mathrm{T}$ then :
question: Bob of a simple pendulum of length $l$ is made of iron. The pendulum is oscillating over a horizontal coil carrying direct current. If the time period of the pendulum is $\mathrm{T}$ then :
options: $T < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is smaller than in air alone.
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone.
$T>2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is smaller than in air alone.
$T < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone.
solution:
When the pendulum is oscillating over a current carrying coil, and when the direction of oscillating pendulum bob is opposite to the direction of current. Its instantaneous acceleration increases.
Hence time period $\mathrm{T} < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{g}}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone due energy dissipation.

question: The function $\sin ^2(\omega t)$ represents

a simple harmonic motion with a period $2 \pi / \omega$
a simple harmonic motion with a period $\pi / \omega$
solution: $y=\frac{(1-\cos 2 \omega t)}{2}$

question: The angular frequency of the damped oscillator is given by, $\omega=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{m}}-\frac{\mathrm{r}^2}{4 \mathrm{~m}^2}\right)}$ where $\mathrm{k}$ is the spring constant, $\mathrm{m}$ is the mass of the oscillator and $r$ is the damping constant. If the ratio $\frac{r^2}{\mathrm{mk}}$ is $8 \%$, the change in time period compared to the undamped oscillator is approximately as follows:
solution: The change in time period compared to the undamped oscillator increases by $1 \%$.

question: A particle of mass $m$ executes simple harmonic motion with amplitude ' $a$ ' and frequency ' $v$ '. The average kinetic energy during its motion from the position of equilibrium to the end is

subject: Physics
q: When the displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is one third of its amplitude, the ratio of total energy to the kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.
question: When the displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is one third of its amplitude, the ratio of total energy to the kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.
solution:
The total energy of the harmonic oscillator is given by the formula

E=12kA2   ...1

where, k is the force constant and A is the amplitude.

The potential energy of the oscillator at the given displacement can be written as

U=12kA32=kA22×9=E9

Hence, its kinetic energy is given by

KE=E-E9=8E9

Thus, the required ratio is

EKE=E8E9=98

Hence, x=9.

q: A particle executes S.H.M., the graph of velocity as a function of displacement is :
question: A particle executes S.H.M., the graph of velocity as a function of displacement is :
solution:
v2=ω2A2-x2

v2ω2+x2=A2
v2ωA2+x2A2=1

This is an equation of an ellipse.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion <math><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> of amplitude <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> along the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis, about <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> When its potential Energy <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>P</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> equal kinetic energy <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>K</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> the position of the particle will be:
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion <math><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> of amplitude <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> along the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis, about <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> When its potential Energy <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>P</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> equal kinetic energy <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>K</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> the position of the particle will be:
solution:
PE=12kx2& KE=12KA2-x2

As given in question

KE=PE

⇒12KA2-x2=12Kx2

⇒2x2=A2

⇒x=±A2

subject: Physics
q: A particle executes simple harmonic motion with an amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. At the mean position, velocity of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The distance of the particle from the mean position when its speed becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion with an amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. At the mean position, velocity of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The distance of the particle from the mean position when its speed becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
The formula to calculate the velocity of the particle at the mean position is given by

V=Aω   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

10=4×ω⇒ω=52 rad s-1

The formula to calculate the velocity of the particle at any instantaneous position x can be expressed as

v=ωA2-x2   ...2

From equation (2), it follows that

5=5242-x2⇒x2=16-4=12⇒x=12 cm

Hence, α=12.

subject: Physics
q: The velocity of a particle executing simple harmonic motion along $x$-axis is described as $v^2 = 50 - x^2$, where $x$ represents displacement. If the time period of motion is $\dfrac{x}{7}$ s, the value of $x$ is _____.
question: The velocity of a particle executing simple harmonic motion along $x$-axis is described as $v^2 = 50 - x^2$, where $x$ represents displacement. If the time period of motion is $\dfrac{x}{7}$ s, the value of $x$ is _____.
solution:
The given equation is $v^2 = 50 - x^2$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$2v \dfrac{dv}{dx} = -2x$

$v \dfrac{dv}{dx} = -x$

Since acceleration $a = v \dfrac{dv}{dx}$, we get:

$a = -x$

Comparing this with the standard equation of SHM $a = -\omega^2 x$, we get:

$\omega^2 = 1 \Rightarrow \omega = 1$ rad/s

The time period of the motion is given by:

$T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi$

Taking $\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}$, we get:

$T = 2 \times \dfrac{22}{7} = \dfrac{44}{7}$ s

Given that the time period is $\dfrac{x}{7}$ s, comparing the two gives:

$x = 44$

Answer: $44$

subject: Physics
q: If the time period of a two meter long simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the acceleration due to gravity at the place where pendulum is executing S.H.M. is:
question: If the time period of a two meter long simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the acceleration due to gravity at the place where pendulum is executing S.H.M. is:
solution:
T=2πl g

2=2π2g

⇒g=2π2

q: In the given figure, a body of mass M is held between two massless springs, on a smooth inclined plane. The free ends of the springs are attached to firm supports. If each spring has spring constant k, the frequency of oscillation of given body is:
question: In the given figure, a body of mass M is held between two massless springs, on a smooth inclined plane. The free ends of the springs are attached to firm supports. If each spring has spring constant k, the frequency of oscillation of given body is:

f=1 T=12π2K m

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass 2 kg is attached with two identical springs of spring constant 20 N m-1 each. The block is placed on a frictionless surface and the ends of the springs are attached to rigid supports (see figure). When the mass is displaced from its equilibrium position, it executes a simple harmonic motion. The time period of oscillation is πX in SI unit. The value of X is______.
question: A block of mass 2 kg is attached with two identical springs of spring constant 20 N m-1 each. The block is placed on a frictionless surface and the ends of the springs are attached to rigid supports (see figure). When the mass is displaced from its equilibrium position, it executes a simple harmonic motion. The time period of oscillation is πX in SI unit. The value of X is______.
solution:
When the block is displaced by a distance x, force acting on block due to both springs will be in the same direction. Therefore,

F=-2kx⇒a=-2kmx,

Comparing it witha=-ω2x , we get

ω=2km=2×202

=20 rad s-1

Now, T=2πω=2π20=π5

Hence, required value of X=5.

subject: Physics
q: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> attached to free end of a spring executes SHM with a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the mass is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> the period of oscillation increases by one second, the value of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>.
question: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> attached to free end of a spring executes SHM with a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the mass is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> the period of oscillation increases by one second, the value of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>.
solution:
Time period of oscillation is given by T=2πmk, where k is the force constant of the spring. 

∵ Spring is same, therefore, spring constant will be same.

Putting the different values,

In the first case          T=1=2πmk

In the second case    T'=2=2πm+3k

 Dividing the equations, we get

 TT'=12=mm+3

⇒mm+3=14⇒4m=m+3⇒m=1

Thus, m=1 kg

subject: Physics
q: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion (SHM). The ratio of potential energy and kinetic energy of the particle when its displacement is half of its amplitude will be
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion (SHM). The ratio of potential energy and kinetic energy of the particle when its displacement is half of its amplitude will be
solution:
The formula to calculate the potential energy of the particle executing SHM is given by

P=12mω2x2   ...1

The formula to calculate the kinetic energy of the same particle is given by

K=12mω2A2-x2   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

PK=12mω2x212mω2A2-x2=x2A2-x2   ...3

Substitute A2 for x into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

PK=A22A2-A22= 13

subject: Physics
q: A silver atom in a solid oscillates in simple harmonic motion in some direction with a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . What is the force constant of the bonds connecting one atom with the other? (Mole wt. of silver,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>108</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and Avogadro number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6.02</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>)
question: A silver atom in a solid oscillates in simple harmonic motion in some direction with a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . What is the force constant of the bonds connecting one atom with the other? (Mole wt. of silver,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>108</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and Avogadro number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6.02</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>)
solution:
Given, molecular weight of silver M=108 g and Avogadro's numberNA=6.02×1023,
frequency f=1012 s.

Mass of single silver atom will be given by,

m=MN=1086.02×1023=1.79×10-22 g=1.79×10-25 kg

The bonds between the atoms can be considered as spring and then using the spring analog, the time period of the oscillation of the atoms is given by,

T=1f=2πmk; where k is spring constant.

11012=2π1.79×10-22×10-3k

k=7.1 N m-1.

subject: Physics
q: The bob of a simple pendulum executes simple harmonic motion in water with a period $t$, while the period of oscillation of the bob is $t_0$ in air. Neglecting frictional force of water and given that the density of the bob is $\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \times 1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. What relationship between $t$ and $t_0$ is true?
question: The bob of a simple pendulum executes simple harmonic motion in water with a period $t$, while the period of oscillation of the bob is $t_0$ in air. Neglecting frictional force of water and given that the density of the bob is $\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \times 1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. What relationship between $t$ and $t_0$ is true?
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}_0}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\left(1-\frac{\rho^{\prime}}{\rho}\right)}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{3}}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}_0}=2$
or, $\mathrm{T}=2 \mathrm{~T}_0$

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a spring is kept in a stretched position with some extension by holding the masses 1 kg and 0.2 kg with a separation more than spring natural length and are released. Assuming the horizontal surface to be frictionless, the angular frequency (in SI unit) of the system is : $k=150 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$
question: As shown in the figure, a spring is kept in a stretched position with some extension by holding the masses 1 kg and 0.2 kg with a separation more than spring natural length and are released. Assuming the horizontal surface to be frictionless, the angular frequency (in SI unit) of the system is : $k=150 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$
solution:
Two masses (1 kg and 0.2 kg) connected by a spring (k = 150 N/m) on a frictionless surface undergo oscillation about their center of mass.
This two-body problem reduces to SHM with reduced mass $\mu = \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{1 \times 0.2}{1.2} = \frac{1}{6}$ kg.
The angular frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} = \sqrt{\frac{150}{1/6}} = \sqrt{900} = 30$ rad/s.

subject: Physics
q: If a simple pendulum has significant amplitude (up to a factor of $1 / e$ of original) only in the period between $t=0s$ to $t=\tau s$, then $\tau$ may be called the average life of the pendulum. When the spherical bob of the pendulum suffers a retardation (due to viscous drag) proportional to its velocity, with '$b$' as the constant of proportionality, the average life time of the pendulum is (assuming damping is small) in seconds:
question: If a simple pendulum has significant amplitude (up to a factor of $1 / e$ of original) only in the period between $t=0s$ to $t=\tau s$, then $\tau$ may be called the average life of the pendulum. When the spherical bob of the pendulum suffers a retardation (due to viscous drag) proportional to its velocity, with '$b$' as the constant of proportionality, the average life time of the pendulum is (assuming damping is small) in seconds:
solution: As retardation $=\mathrm{bv}$ $\therefore$ retarding force $=\mathrm{mbv}$ $\therefore$ net restoring torque when angular displacement is $\theta$ is given by $=-\operatorname{mg} \ell \sin \theta+\operatorname{mbv} \ell$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{I} \alpha=-m g \ell \sin \theta+\operatorname{mbv} \ell$ where, $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{m} \ell^2$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \theta}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=\alpha=-\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell} \sin \theta+\frac{\mathrm{bv}}{\ell}$ for small damping, the solution of the above differential equation will be $\therefore \quad \theta=\theta_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{\mathrm{bt}}{2}} \sin (w t+\phi)$ $\therefore \quad$ angular amplitude will be $=\theta . e^{\frac{-b t}{2}}$ According to question, in $\tau$ time (average life-time), angular amplitude drops to $\frac{1}{e}$ value of its original value $(\theta)$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\theta_0}{\mathrm{e}}=\theta_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{6 \tau}{2}}$ $\frac{6 \tau}{2}=1$ $\therefore \quad \tau=\frac{2}{b}$

subject: Physics
q: Two blocks with masses 100 g and 200 g are attached to the ends of springs $A$ and $B$ as shown in figure. The energy stored in $A$ is $E$. The energy stored in $B$, when spring constants $k_{A}, k_{B}$ of $A$ and $B$, respectively satisfy the relation $4 k_{A}=3 k_{B^{\prime}}$, is :
question: Two blocks with masses 100 g and 200 g are attached to the ends of springs $A$ and $B$ as shown in figure. The energy stored in $A$ is $E$. The energy stored in $B$, when spring constants $k_{A}, k_{B}$ of $A$ and $B$, respectively satisfy the relation $4 k_{A}=3 k_{B^{\prime}}$, is :
solution:
For blocks hanging in equilibrium, spring extensions are:
$x_A = \frac{m_A g}{k_A} = \frac{100g}{k_A}$ and $x_B = \frac{m_B g}{k_B} = \frac{200g}{k_B}$.
Energy stored:
$E = \frac{1}{2}k_A x_A^2 = \frac{5000g^2}{k_A}$ and $U_B = \frac{1}{2}k_B x_B^2 = \frac{20000g^2}{k_B}$.
Taking ratio: $\frac{U_B}{E} = 4 \cdot \frac{k_A}{k_B}$.
Given $4k_A = 3k_B$, we have $\frac{k_A}{k_B} = \frac{3}{4}$.
Therefore: $U_B = 4E \cdot \frac{3}{4} = 3E$.

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum of string length 30 cm performs 20 oscillations in 10 s. The length of the string required for the pendulum to perform 40 oscillations in the same time duration is $\_\_\_\_$ cm. [Assume that the mass of the pendulum remains same.]
question: A simple pendulum of string length 30 cm performs 20 oscillations in 10 s. The length of the string required for the pendulum to perform 40 oscillations in the same time duration is $\_\_\_\_$ cm. [Assume that the mass of the pendulum remains same.]
solution:
Period of simple pendulum: $T = \frac{\text{time}}{\text{number of oscillations}}$
Initial period: $T_1 = \frac{10}{20} = 0.5$ s
Required period: $T_2 = \frac{10}{40} = 0.25$ s
Using $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$, the ratio is: $\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}}$
$\frac{0.25}{0.5} = \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{30}} \Rightarrow 0.5 = \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{30}}$
$0.25 = \frac{L_2}{30} \Rightarrow L_2 = 7.5$ cm

subject: Physics
q: The point A moves with a uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius 0.36 m and covers 30° in 0.1 s. The perpendicular projection P from A on the diameter MN represents the simple harmonic motion of P. The restoration force per unit mass when P touches M will be :
question: The point A moves with a uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius 0.36 m and covers 30° in 0.1 s. The perpendicular projection P from A on the diameter MN represents the simple harmonic motion of P. The restoration force per unit mass when P touches M will be :
solution:
The point A covers 30° in 0.1 s.
Then, we have π6 rad⟶0.1 s.
Or 1 rad⟶0.1π6 s
Or 2π in→ 0.1×6π×2π
Thus, time taken is T=1.2 sec.

We know that ω=2πT
Then, ω=2π1.2
Restoration force (F)=mω2A.

Then Restoration force per unit mass Fm=ω2 A
Fm=2π1.22×0.36

≅9.87 N

question:
Consider two identical springs each of spring constant k and negligible mass compared to the mass M as shown. Fig. 1 shows one of them and Fig. 2 shows their series combination. The ratios of time period of oscillation of the two SHM is TbTa=x, where value of x is ______.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer)
solution:
Ta=2πMK

Tb=2πMK/2

TbTa=2=x

At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, it is at its position of equilibrium. The kinetic energy - time graph of the particle will look like:
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with a time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, it is at its position of equilibrium. The kinetic energy - time graph of the particle will look like:
solution:
x=Asin⁡ω t

v=dxdt=a ωcos⁡ω t

K.E.=12m v2

=12 m A2ω2cos2⁡ ωt

=12 m A2ω2cos2⁡2πT t

At t=0, K.E. is maximum.

The period of K.E. graph is half of the period of displacement graph.

Comment:- The points on the graph of x-axis should be differentiable.

In all option mentioned, the graph has cusps at the points on x-axis.

q: A bob of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> suspended by a thread of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ℓ</mi></math> undergoes simple harmonic oscillations with time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the bob is immersed in a liquid that has density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> times that of the bob and the length of the thread is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mi>d</mi></mrow></msup></math> of the original length, then the time period of the simple harmonic oscillations will be:
question: A bob of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> suspended by a thread of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ℓ</mi></math> undergoes simple harmonic oscillations with time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the bob is immersed in a liquid that has density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> times that of the bob and the length of the thread is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mi>d</mi></mrow></msup></math> of the original length, then the time period of the simple harmonic oscillations will be:
solution:
T=2πℓg
geff=g-g4=3g4  ∴ T'=2π4ℓ/33g/4=2π16ℓ9g
T'=43 T

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical block of mass $M$ and area of cross section $A$ is floating in a liquid of density $\rho$ and with its axis vertical. When depressed a little and released the block starts oscillating. The period of oscillation is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A cylindrical block of mass $M$ and area of cross section $A$ is floating in a liquid of density $\rho$ and with its axis vertical. When depressed a little and released the block starts oscillating. The period of oscillation is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
When the block is depressed by distance $x$, the additional buoyant force is $F = \rho Agx$ acting upward as restoring force.
Equation of motion: $M\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -\rho Agx$.
This gives $\omega^2 = \frac{\rho Ag}{M}$.
Period of oscillation: $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{M}{\rho Ag}}$

subject: Physics
q: The time period of a simple harmonic oscillator is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$. Measured value of mass $(m)$ of the object is 10 g with an accuracy of 10 mg and time for 50 oscillations of the spring is found to be 60 s using a watch of 2 s resolution. Percentage error in determination of spring constant $(k)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ \%.
question: The time period of a simple harmonic oscillator is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$. Measured value of mass $(m)$ of the object is 10 g with an accuracy of 10 mg and time for 50 oscillations of the spring is found to be 60 s using a watch of 2 s resolution. Percentage error in determination of spring constant $(k)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ \%.
solution:
The given formula for time period is
$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$.
Squaring both sides, we get $T^2 = 4\pi^2 \frac{k}{m}$, which gives $k = \frac{4\pi^2 m}{T^2}$.
The relative error in $k$ is given by $\frac{\Delta k}{k} = \frac{\Delta m}{m} + 2\frac{\Delta T}{T}$.
Given: $m = 10$ g, $\Delta m = 10$ mg $= 0.01$ g.
Total time for $n=50$ oscillations is $t = 60$ s with resolution $\Delta t = 2$ s.
Since $T = t/n$, the relative error in $T$ is the same as in $t$: $\frac{\Delta T}{T} = \frac{\Delta t}{t}$.
Substituting the values: $\frac{\Delta k}{k} = \frac{0.01}{10} + 2\left(\frac{2}{60}\right)$.
$\frac{\Delta k}{k} = 0.001 + \frac{2}{30} = 0.001 + 0.0666...$
Percentage error $= \left(0.001 + \frac{1}{15}\right) \times 100 = 0.1 + \frac{100}{15} = 0.1 + 6.666... = 6.766...\%$.
Rounding to two decimal places, we get $6.76\%$.
